headline: Sarah’s clarity is very low because she can name the influence gap, but she still describes a late-socializing pattern that undermines Senior-level impact and visibility.
summary_bullets: Sarah frames her Senior gap as “influence is uneven” and repeats the same mechanism: “wait until analysis is complete before socializing direction.”
She names a specific stakeholder failure mode—“stakeholders feel surprised late”—but her plan stays at process level (“define what good looks like”) rather than observable influence behaviors.
She wants “lead one cross-team decision this cycle, end to end,” yet her own leverage choice is “feedback checkpoints during projects,” which risks still centering her execution timeline.
understanding_paragraph: Sarah understands her performance problem precisely: her technical output is solid, but she is not shaping decisions early enough. The session clarified that her influence gap is driven by how she times direction-sharing—she waits for analysis completion, which directly causes stakeholders to feel surprised late. She also has a concrete aspiration (own one cross-team decision end to end), but the current commitments don’t yet translate into clear, early, stakeholder-visible behaviors.

axes.meaning: Weak signal only: wellbeing doesn’t show up as a driver in this session, so it’s not actionable.
No clear signal on motivation or ownership beyond acknowledging the influence gap; watch for follow-through on the next commitments.
A defining clarity problem: she can describe the issue, but her described approach still delays stakeholder alignment and visibility.
Worth noting but not decisive: growth intent exists (“checkpoints”), yet the plan needs stronger feedback loops tied to influence outcomes.

brutal_truth_employee: Sarah is honest that “my influence is uneven” and that she “wait[s] until analysis is complete before socializing direction.” She also states the direct consequence: “stakeholders feel surprised late.” That pattern will keep blocking Senior-level impact unless she changes how early she commits direction and narrative with non-technical partners.
brutal_truth_manager: When Sarah said “I tend to wait until analysis is complete before socializing direction,” the conversation should have forced a concrete behavior change: what exact early artifact or decision point she will socialize, to whom, and by when. The manager let the discussion land on process (“define what good looks like”) without pinning it to observable stakeholder influence moments.

next_actions.action: Confirm with Sarah the exact early decision point she will socialize (date + audience) for her next cross-team decision she wants to lead end to end.
Schedule a 30-minute stakeholder pre-wire with Sarah: she drafts a one-page “tradeoffs narrative” and shares it before analysis completion.
Review one named project: ask Sarah to show evidence of early shaping (who was influenced, what decision changed, and when).

watch_for: Before next 1:1: if Sarah still describes direction-sharing as “after analysis is complete,” treat clarity and influence as unchanged.
Within two weeks: if stakeholders still report “surprised late” moments, require an earlier narrative checkpoint with named audiences.
